title: Angular Routing
summary: Route configuration, lazy loading, route guards, resolvers, and navigation with Angular Router.
guide:
# Angular Routing

Angular Router maps URL paths to components — first-match wins from top to bottom. Think of the routes array as a priority-ordered table: Angular walks it until a path matches, then renders that component into the `<router-outlet>`.

```
URL: /admin/users/42
  ↓
Routes table (top-to-bottom, first match)
  { path: '' }         ✗ no match
  { path: 'about' }    ✗ no match
  { path: 'admin',     ✓  (lazy module loaded)
    children: [
      { path: 'users/:id' } ✓  ProductDetail rendered
    ]
  }
  { path: '**' }       ← never reached (MUST be last)
```

## Route Config
```typescript
export const routes: Routes = [
  { path: '',           component: HomeComponent },
  { path: 'products/:id', component: ProductDetailComponent },
  { path: 'admin', canActivate: [authGuard],
    loadChildren: () => import('./admin/admin.routes').then(m => m.adminRoutes) },
  { path: '**', component: NotFoundComponent }  // MUST be last
];
```

## Lazy Loading
```typescript
{ path: 'dashboard', loadComponent: () =>
    import('./dashboard.component').then(m => m.DashboardComponent) }
```

## Functional Guards (Angular 14+)
```typescript
export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => {
  const auth = inject(AuthService);
  return auth.isLoggedIn()
    ? true
    : inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login'], { queryParams: { returnUrl: state.url } });
};
```

## Reading Route Params
```typescript
// One-time (snapshot):
const id = this.route.snapshot.paramMap.get('id');
// Reactive (same component, different params):
this.route.paramMap.pipe(map(p => p.get('id')))
```

```
snapshot frozen at init time
  /product/1 → component created → snapshot.id = '1'
  user clicks /product/2:
    Angular reuses component (no destroy)
    snapshot still = '1'  ← BUG
  Solution: subscribe to paramMap Observable
```

## Resolvers
```typescript
export const productResolver: ResolveFn<Product> = (route) =>
  inject(ProductService).getProduct(route.paramMap.get('id')!);
// Route: { resolve: { product: productResolver } }
// Component: inject(ActivatedRoute).snapshot.data['product']
```

## withPreloading
```typescript
provideRouter(routes, withPreloading(PreloadAllModules))
// Loads lazy modules in background after initial load
// Custom strategy: implement PreloadingStrategy interface
```

## Common Pitfalls
- `**` wildcard must be LAST.
- Guard returning `false` shows blank page — always return a UrlTree for redirect.
- Use `route.paramMap` Observable when same component navigates to different params.
- Child routes render in the PARENT component's `<router-outlet>`.
- NgModule apps: `RouterModule.forRoot(routes)`. Standalone: `provideRouter(routes)` in bootstrapApplication.
- `pathMatch: 'full'` required for empty path + redirectTo, otherwise `/` matches every URL.
- `RouterModule.forRoot` in a lazy module creates a second Router instance — only use `forChild` in feature modules.

## Named Router Outlets
```typescript
// Template:
<router-outlet></router-outlet>               // primary
<router-outlet name="sidebar"></router-outlet> // secondary

// Navigate:
router.navigate([{ outlets: { sidebar: ['help'] } }]);
```

## Query Params vs Route Params
```typescript
// /products?sort=price&dir=asc
route.queryParamMap.subscribe(p => p.get('sort'));  // 'price'

// /products/:id  →  /products/42
route.paramMap.subscribe(p => p.get('id'));  // '42'

// Fragment /products#reviews
route.fragment.subscribe(f => f);  // 'reviews'
```

choices: Angular matches top-to-bottom — wildcard first catches all URLs
questions.explanation: First-match strategy: a wildcard route first would swallow all paths.

choices: Smaller initial bundle — route code downloads on first visit
questions.explanation: Lazy loading splits the bundle. Rarely-visited pages (admin, reports) don't inflate the initial download.

choices: When same component navigates to different params without being destroyed
questions.explanation: Snapshot is frozen at init. If Angular reuses the component for new params, snapshot stays stale.

choices: Blocks navigation — page stays blank unless redirected
questions.explanation: false blocks without redirecting. Use createUrlTree(['/login']) to properly redirect.
Array segments become path parts: /orders/42/confirm.

choices: Pre-fetches data before component activates — available synchronously in snapshot.data
questions.explanation: Resolver ensures data is ready before the view renders.
provideRouter(routes) is the functional standalone replacement.
canActivate, canDeactivate, canActivateChild, canMatch are real guards. canDestroy doesn't exist.

questions.prompt: Child routes render in which outlet?
questions.explanation: Nested routes: parent must have its own <router-outlet> in its template.
canDeactivate fires when leaving. Used for unsaved-form confirmation.

questions.prompt: Why is `pathMatch: 'full'` required on `{ path: '', redirectTo: '/home' }`?
choices: Empty path is deprecated without it
Without it, '' matches every URL (prefix match), causing all routes to redirect
questions.explanation: By default Angular uses prefix matching. '' is a prefix of every URL, so without pathMatch:'full' every navigation triggers the redirect.

questions.prompt: Where does a child route's component render?
choices: In the parent route's component template's <router-outlet>
questions.explanation: Nested routes require the parent component to have its own <router-outlet>. The child renders there, not in the root outlet.

choices: Loads all modules before the app starts
After the initial load, downloads lazy modules in background so future navigations are instant
questions.explanation: Preloading is a background optimization. The app still only executes modules when navigated to, but the JS is already downloaded.

questions.prompt: User navigates from /products/1 to /products/2. Component stays alive. What is the snapshot id?
choices: '1' — snapshot frozen at component creation time
questions.explanation: snapshot is taken once at component activation. If Angular reuses the component for a new param, snapshot is stale. Subscribe to paramMap Observable instead.

questions.prompt: Which things does a Resolver do?
choices: Fetch data before the component activates
Make data available synchronously in snapshot.data
questions.explanation: Resolvers pre-fetch data (guaranteed available in snapshot.data). They do NOT guard access — that's canActivate. They can cancel by returning EMPTY.

choices: Before any child route of the parent activates — protecting an entire section
Same as canActivate on every child
questions.explanation: canActivateChild is placed on the parent route and fires before any of its children activate, without listing the guard on each child individually.
Array syntax: each element is a path segment. Variables are interpolated. Result: /user/42/details.

questions.prompt: canMatch guard differs from canActivate because:
choices: canMatch runs before Angular matches the route entry at all
canMatch only works on lazy routes
questions.explanation: canMatch gates the route matching phase. If it returns false/UrlTree, Angular continues to the next route definition. Enables role-based route tables.

questions.prompt: authGuard returns `false` for an unauthenticated user navigating to /dashboard. What does the user see?
questions.explanation: false simply blocks navigation with no redirect. The URL may update but no component renders. Always return a UrlTree to redirect.

choices: forChild creates a second Router service
forRoot registers the Router service once; forChild registers additional routes without a second service
questions.explanation: forRoot creates the singleton Router + directive providers. forChild adds routes to the existing router. Calling forRoot in a lazy module creates a second Router — breaks app.

flashcards.back: snapshot: frozen at init. paramMap: reactive stream. Use Observable when same component navigates between different IDs.
{ path: 'p', loadComponent: () => import('./p.component').then(m => m.PComponent) }. Separate JS chunk downloaded on first visit.
true/false. UrlTree (createUrlTree(['/login'])): redirect. Observable<boolean|UrlTree>: async. Never return false alone.
bootstrapApplication(App, { providers: [provideRouter(routes, withPreloading(...))] }). Functional and composable.
ResolveFn<T> = (route) => inject(Service).fetch(id). Route: resolve: { key: resolverFn }. Component reads: snapshot.data['key'].
const g: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => { const a = inject(Auth); return a.loggedIn() ? true : inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login']); }
Required on empty path redirects. Without it, '' prefix-matches every URL. With it, only matches exactly ''. Essential for { path: '', redirectTo: '/home' }.
canActivate: blocks after route matched. canMatch: fires before matching — false makes Angular skip to next route definition. Enables role-partitioned route tables.

project.brief: Design routing for a task app: public (/, /login), protected (dashboard, tasks, task-detail with resolver+canDeactivate), admin (role-guarded, lazy). Write the routes array and functional auth+role guards.

checklist.text: Public routes eager; protected with canActivate: [authGuard]
Tasks and admin loaded via loadChildren (separate routes files)
Task detail: resolve: { task: taskResolver }, canDeactivate: [unsavedGuard]
authGuard returns createUrlTree(['/login'], { queryParams: { returnUrl: state.url } })
Admin guard reads user.role and returns createUrlTree(['/forbidden']) if not admin

hints: Functional guard: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => inject(Auth).isLoggedIn() ? true : inject(Router).createUrlTree(['/login', ...])
returnUrl: after login use this.router.navigateByUrl(route.queryParams['returnUrl']) to restore destination.
Routes file (not module): export const taskRoutes: Routes = [...]. loadChildren: () => import('./task.routes').then(m => m.taskRoutes).
